text:
Climate is one of the important influencing factors for the transmission of infectious diseases, and any changes in it can impact natural ecosystems and socio-economic systems. Schistosomiasis is a serious infectious parasitic disease that poses a significant threat to human health and is zoonotic. In China, schistosomiasis mainly occurs in the lake regions of five provinces along the Yangtze River: Hubei, Hunan, Jiangxi, Anhui, and Jiangsu. Among these, the 11 endemic areas in Jiangxi Province are primarily located in the Poyang Lake region and parts of the Gan River area. In recent years, the schistosomiasis epidemic in the Poyang Lake basin has shown a trend of spreading, with endemic areas distributed across 160 towns and villages in 8 counties along the lake, affecting a population of over 2 million people, which directly impacts national economy and people's livelihoods, severely restricting the socio-economic development of the Poyang Lake ecological economic zone. 

The distribution and transmission of schistosomiasis are closely related to climate factors such as temperature, light, rainfall, and humidity, showing a distinct regional pattern. The only intermediate host of schistosomiasis, the Oncomelania snail, directly influences the prevalence and spread of the disease. In nature, Oncomelania snails are most active from April to June and from September to October. Temperature and light primarily affect the reproduction of the intermediate host snails, the development of the schistosome larvae within the snails, and the survival of the larvae in the environment (including the hatching of miracidia and the emergence of cercariae). Rainfall and humidity affect the distribution of snail breeding habitats, and the geographical distribution of snails largely determines the spatial distribution of schistosomes. The distribution of cercariae in the waters of Poyang Lake exhibits significant seasonal variation. This study selected 8 sites in the schistosomiasis endemic areas of Poyang Lake, including Nanchang, Xinjian, Jinxian, Poyang, Yugan, Yongxiu, Xingzi, and Duchang, to collect data on snail control statistics, measured temperature, precipitation, etc., and constructed a climate change-schistosomiasis transmission index prediction model to forecast the transmission of schistosomiasis. The Lorenz curve and Theil index were used to analyze the spatial variation of snail-infested areas, combined with GIS technology and statistical software such as Excel and SPSS to analyze the temporal and spatial distribution characteristics of schistosomiasis in the Poyang Lake area under the background of climate change.

The research results indicate that against the backdrop of climate change, the rapidly growing population affected by schistosomiasis in the Poyang Lake area is closely related to the changes in temperature and precipitation within the counties of the region, showing a positive correlation with temperature and an inverse relationship with precipitation. The transmission of schistosomiasis in the Poyang Lake area not only exhibits significant differences over time but also shows considerable spatial variation within the counties of the lake region. The suitable habitats for Oncomelania snails and schistosomes in the Poyang Lake area have shifted northward, potentially causing the transmission of schistosomiasis to also shift northward, further affecting the spread of the epidemic in the Poyang Lake area. 

The findings will provide a decision-making basis for the social and economic development and ecological environmental protection of the Poyang Lake ecological economic zone, and may also serve as a reference model for the prevention and control of schistosomiasis in lakes and marshes across Jiangxi and the entire country.

text:
Schistosomiasis is a zoonotic parasitic disease that is naturally endemic and localized. The eight counties along the shores of Poyang Lake have not yet interrupted the transmission of schistosomiasis, posing significant threats to the physical and mental health of the population in endemic areas, as well as to economic development and social progress. Entering the 21st century, the incidence and infection rates of schistosomiasis have reached historically low levels, yet there remains a high risk of infection in certain susceptible areas. The geographical characteristics of these susceptible zones are complex and diverse, with high infection risks, frequent human and animal activities, and numerous uncertainties in research. The comprehensive application of ground surveys, GIS, and other geoinformation technologies for studying schistosomiasis susceptible areas is a new and effective approach. 

Relevant studies indicate that the formation of schistosomiasis susceptible areas is closely related to factors such as climate, hydrology, vegetation, soil, elevation, cultural background, lifestyle habits, and socioeconomic conditions. These susceptible areas are characterized by concentrated distribution of animal feces, high density of infectious snails, high density of cercariae in water, frequent human and animal activities, and high infection rates. They represent regions where natural environmental and socioeconomic factors related to schistosomiasis transmission spatially overlap, resulting in high infection risks. These areas primarily cluster along the interface of land and water, exhibiting a certain stability in spatial distribution and seasonal variations in susceptibility.

The distribution range of schistosomiasis endemic areas around Poyang Lake is extensive, with significant spatial differences in prevalence. The spatial differentiation of macro topography and hydrological conditions serves as the main basis for the geographical classification of schistosomiasis susceptible areas. Differences in culture, society, and economy among various types of endemic areas lead to spatial variations in human-animal contact with contaminated water, awareness levels regarding schistosomiasis prevention, and control measures, resulting in this non-zonal geographical differentiation. The schistosomiasis susceptible areas around Poyang Lake can be categorized into four types: floodplain, embankment, island, and lake bay types. Field investigations into the geographical characteristics of susceptible areas and sampling typical natural villages for epidemiological surveys are conducted, with Nanchang County as a case study to analyze the spatiotemporal distribution characteristics of schistosomiasis. 

Results indicate that there are significant differences in the geomorphological and hydrological combinations of different types of susceptible areas, with complex and diverse epidemiological characteristics, though the differences are relatively minor. Villages with schistosomiasis in Nanchang County are closer to grass floodplains, resulting in higher infection rates; the closer the distance to snail-infested grass floodplains, the higher the density of infectious snails. From 2004 to 2009, the epidemic showed a declining trend, yet high-risk susceptible areas still existed, exhibiting clustered distribution with unstable changes in local epidemics over the years. 

By employing a combination of qualitative and quantitative spatial analysis methods, a knowledge base and geographical spatial model were established to explore the spatial correlation between schistosomiasis susceptible areas and related geographical entities, enabling knowledge-driven spatial distribution simulations of susceptible areas in Nanchang County. Results show that the spatial distribution of susceptible areas along the lakeshore grass floodplains in Nanchang County is concentrated and contiguous. Beyond the embankments, there are extensive potential high-risk areas for schistosomiasis infection. High-risk susceptible areas primarily exhibit point or block distributions along the embankments outside the dikes, particularly in coastal administrative villages such as Yufeng Village, Wufeng Village, Jiangjunzhou, and Wuxing Farm, where residential areas are in close proximity to high-risk susceptible zones, leading to a higher risk of infection.

text:
"The 'Neglected Tropical Diseases' are a group of chronic disabling diseases widely prevalent in developing countries, particularly in impoverished and remote areas, as defined by the World Health Organization. In recent years, research has focused on comprehensive prevention and control measures based on social and biological factors related to these diseases. In 2011, the Regional Cooperation Organization for Schistosomiasis and Other Zoonotic Parasitic Diseases in Asia (RNAS+) initiated a research project funded by the Canadian Development and Research Center, aimed at 'Developing Innovative Strategies for Sustainable Control of Tropical Diseases Based on Socio-Ecological Measures.' The goal is to explore the comprehensive social and ecological factors affecting tropical diseases and to establish integrated socio-ecological measures for their prevention and control. This study takes schistosomiasis as a specific disease case, examining the distribution and influencing factors of schistosomiasis at different scales. Research on the factors influencing schistosomiasis in lake and water network areas in China is relatively abundant, but studies in hilly schistosomiasis endemic areas are limited. Therefore, this research collects geographical environmental data (remote sensing data, vector maps), disease-related data (snail infection data, population survey data), and socio-economic data (individual-level knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors regarding schistosomiasis) to study the distribution and influencing factors of schistosomiasis from large-scale, hilly endemic areas, and individual levels. The entire study is divided into four chapters:

The first part involves the preliminary ecological zoning of large-scale areas. Objective: To attempt a methodological approach to large-scale ecological zoning based on multidimensional environmental factors, providing ideas for monitoring point establishment and cross-national cooperation for schistosomiasis. Method: The study area includes Vietnam, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, the Philippines, and the provinces of Yunnan, Guangdong, and Guangxi in China. Data on surface temperature and normalized difference vegetation index from July 2002 to July 2011 were collected, and a two-factor clustering analysis was employed to establish different ecological zone systems. Results: The study area was divided into five ecological zones, located as follows: Zone A is distributed in central Guangxi, northern Vietnam, central and western Thailand, and northwestern Philippines; Zone B is found in Laos, southwestern Cambodia, southwestern Thailand, and western Philippines; Zone C is in eastern Yunnan, central and southern Guangdong, central and eastern Thailand, and southwestern Cambodia; Zone D is in northern Guangxi and Guangdong, northern Vietnam, northwestern Thailand, and western Philippines; Zone E is in western Yunnan, central Guangxi, northeastern Cambodia, northwestern Thailand, and central western Philippines. The risk of schistosomiasis distribution varies among different ecological zones. Conclusion: This ecological zoning method is theoretically feasible and can be expanded for use, providing new ideas for monitoring point establishment and cross-national cooperation for tropical diseases like schistosomiasis.

The second part analyzes the spatiotemporal characteristics of Oncomelania snails in hilly schistosomiasis endemic areas. Objective: To study the spatial distribution characteristics of Oncomelania snails in Eryuan County from 2005 to 2012 and their temporal trends. Method: Based on vector maps of Eryuan County in Yunnan Province, data on snail surveys (snail presence rate, average density of live snails, density of positive snails, natural infection rate) from 2005 to 2012 were collected. Spatial autocorrelation analysis, local autocorrelation, kernel density analysis, and 'hotspot' analysis methods were used to explore the spatiotemporal distribution and trends of Oncomelania snails. Results: Live snails in Eryuan County were primarily distributed in four areas: Yonglian Village in the central region, Qiye Village in the southwest, and Jiangdeng Village in the southeast, with the distribution range shrinking and density decreasing annually. Positive snails were concentrated around Yonglian Village from 2005 to 2007. There was spatial autocorrelation in the distribution of Oncomelania snails from 2005 to 2012, with an increasing trend in correlation over time. High-value clustering areas increased annually, mainly concentrated in mountainous canyon endemic areas, such as near Lian Tie Village. Further 'hotspot' analysis revealed that the aggregation areas of live Oncomelania snails in Eryuan County were near Xinzhuang, Lian Tie, Yongsheng, and Yongle Villages. Conclusion: The distribution of Oncomelania snails in Eryuan County exhibits spatial and local autocorrelation, with 'hotspot areas' identified, providing a basis for future snail control efforts in Eryuan County.

The third part investigates the environmental factors of Oncomelania snail breeding habitats in mountainous areas and makes predictions. Objective: To study the geographical environmental factors affecting Oncomelania snail breeding habitats in Eryuan County and to establish a suitability model using remote sensing data combined with historical snail data for predicting potential breeding habitats. Method: Data on the minimum surface temperature (LSTN), normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), digital elevation model (DEM), and derived hydrological models from July 2002 to July 2011 were collected. Historical data from administrative villages with snail presence were sampled to preliminarily understand the environmental characteristics of snail breeding habitats. Suitable environmental factors were classified and overlaid to establish a suitability model, predicting the distribution of Oncomelania snails in hilly areas and validating the model. Results: 96.4% of administrative villages with snails were located within 1200m of water systems, 79% were in the nighttime temperature range of -0.15℃ to 4.85℃, 75% were in the NDVI range of 0.38 to 0.7; 71.4% were at elevations between 1950 to 2491m; and 75% were on slopes between 1° to 20°. The suitable range was classified, overlaid, and potential breeding habitats were extracted, with the model's sensitivity at 85% and specificity at 75%. Conclusion: Based on LSTN, NDVI, DEM, and derived hydrological features, a suitability model for predicting Oncomelania snails can be established for forecasting in endemic areas.

The fourth part studies the social influencing factors of schistosomiasis at the individual level. Objective: To understand the social influencing factors of schistosomiasis in different sub-epidemic areas of Eryuan County from an individual perspective, providing a basis for formulating comprehensive prevention and control measures tailored to local conditions. Method: A cross-sectional survey combining questionnaires and in-depth interviews was conducted in two representative administrative villages of schistosomiasis (Yongle and Xinzhuang) in Eryuan County, assessing the population's knowledge, behaviors, attitudes related to schistosomiasis, and infection status. Univariate analysis and binary logistic regression models were used to analyze the correlation between schistosomiasis infection rates and individual knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors regarding schistosomiasis, exploring risk factors in the two sub-epidemic areas. Results: Yongle Village (plain type) had previously higher schistosomiasis infection rates than Xinzhuang (canyon type), but currently, Xinzhuang's infection rate is higher, related to the presence of more risk factors in Xinzhuang, such as frequent cattle raising, foraging for wild vegetables, and a free-range attitude towards livestock. Additionally, the awareness rate among children under 15 is lower than that of other groups, failing to meet national standards, and there are many risky behaviors, such as swimming in the wild, especially in Xinzhuang. Conclusion: Overall awareness of schistosomiasis in Eryuan County is relatively high, but different risk factors exist in the two sub-epidemic areas. Current low-prevalence areas require tailored comprehensive prevention and control measures based on research priorities, particularly health education measures targeting children under 15."

text: Objective: (1) To analyze the epidemic status and trend of schistosomiasis in the Jiujiang region of the Poyang Lake Ecological Economic Zone, providing a reference for setting prevention and control goals and adjusting strategies for schistosomiasis control. (2) To explore the feasibility of applying a gray model to predict the schistosomiasis epidemic in this region, providing methodological support for establishing an early warning system for schistosomiasis in the Poyang Lake Ecological Economic Zone. Methods: (1) Collect annual reports on schistosomiasis prevention and control in the Jiujiang region from 2003 to 2015, and analyze data on human and livestock infections and snail populations using statistical methods such as trend chi-square tests. (2) Based on schistosomiasis epidemic data from 2005 to 2014, establish an original model using the gray system GM(1,1) model, and employ an equal-dimensional gray number dynamic prediction model to forecast the development trend of schistosomiasis from 2015 to 2020. Introduce environmental interference factors to adjust the prediction results, and compare the actual data of schistosomiasis indicators in 2015 with the predicted values to evaluate the model's predictive effectiveness. Results: (1) From 2003 to 2015, the number of counties in the Jiujiang region of the Poyang Lake Ecological Economic Zone with interrupted transmission increased from 2 to 4, and the number of counties under control increased from 5 to 9, with the number of counties not achieving control reduced to 0. The positive rates of blood and stool tests in the population significantly decreased, with the infection rate of schistosomiasis dropping sharply (all below 0.01%). The number of acute infection cases fell rapidly from 49 to 0, while the number of new late-stage schistosomiasis cases gradually increased from 2003 to 2010, then declined after 2011, showing a fluctuating downward trend. The positive rate in cattle decreased from 3.74% in 2003 to 0.07% in 2015, a reduction of 98.13%. The area of snails remained between 24,000 and 24,800 hm² from 2005 to 2015, with a slow increasing trend, while the area treated for snail control increased by 59.54%. (2) Predictive models were established for the population infection rate, cattle positive rate, and snail area, showing good fitting results. The medium- and long-term predictions indicate that the expected population infection rates for schistosomiasis from 2016 to 2020 are 0.0078%, 0.0021%, 0.0003%, 0.0003%, and 0.0002%, respectively; the expected positive rates for cattle are 0.1466%, 0.0580%, 0.0208%, 0.0214%, and 0.0249%; and the expected snail areas are 24,850.69 hm², 24,915.83 hm², 24,974.10 hm², 25,030.61 hm², and 25,124.46 hm². The overall trend for the population infection rate and cattle positive rate is downward in the coming years, while the snail area shows a slow growth trend. Conclusion: The schistosomiasis epidemic in the Jiujiang region of the Poyang Lake Ecological Economic Zone shows an overall downward trend, although there is a possibility of an increase in snail area; the gray prediction model demonstrates good predictive effectiveness for schistosomiasis in this area and can be used for medium- and long-term predictive analysis of the epidemic.
